```jl
julia> v = Union{Vector{Int}, Nothing}[Vector{Int}[] for _ in 1:3]
```
This returns a `Vector{Union{Vector{Int}, Nothing}}` but, really, I think it should error. The issue is that the elements in `[Vector{Int}[] for _ in 1:3]` are _not_ `Vector{Int}` but `Vector{Vector{Int}}`, so we have scenario of the kind `Union{A, B}[c::C for c in ...]` with `C` not a subtype of `A` or `B`.

The reason this works is that each of the `Vector{Int}[]` elements are passed on to the conversion machinery. In particular, as pointed out by @thofma on Slack, this is probably passed to one of these methods:

```jl
julia> convert(Vector{Int}, Vector{Int}[]) # Int64[]
julia> Vector{Int}(Vector{Int}[]) # Int64[]
```

It's hard to see where this would be desirable though. Additionally, the conversion only works if the to-be-converted-array is empty. That is, the following fails:
```jl
julia> convert(Vector{Int}, Vector{Int}[[1,2,3],[4,5]])
julia> Vector{Int}(Vector{Int}[[1,2,3],[4,5]])
```
It seems strange and error-prone to have conversion methods that only work for empty input

```jl

```
This returns a `Vector{Union{Vector{Int}, Nothing}}` but, really, I think it should error. The issue is that the elements in `[Vector{Int}[] for _ in 1:3]` are _not_ `Vector{Int}` but `Vector{Vector{Int}}`, so we have scenario of the kind `Union{A, B}[c::C for c in ...]` with `C` not a subtype of `A` or `B`.

The reason this works is that each of the `Vector{Int}[]` elements are passed on to the conversion machinery. In particular, as pointed out by @thofma on Slack, this is probably passed to one of these methods:

```jl
julia> convert(Vector{Int}, Vector{Int}[]) # Int64[]

It's hard to see where this would be desirable though. Additionally, the conversion only works if the to-be-converted-array is empty. That is, the following fails:

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568","id":2660553081,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6elNF5","number":56568,"title":"how to define a more general `unique` function conveniently?","user":{"login":"WalterMadelim","id":143272683,"node_id":"U_kgDOCIoq6w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/143272683?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim","html_url":"https://github.com/WalterMadelim","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-11-15T02:54:17Z","updated_at":"2024-11-15T07:59:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"`unique` function is based on `isequal` function.
If `isequal` function can be user-defined, we can also get a resultant new `unique` function. This should be convenient. 
But how to achieve this? Currently I don't know the way. See the following code.

```julia
julia> isequal(2, 3)
false

julia> unique([2, 3, 5, 2])   # 3 is different from 2, hence it remains
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 2
 3
 5

julia> Base.isequal(m::Int, n::Int) = ( abs(m - n) <= 1 ? true : false )  # user-define a new method

julia> isequal(2, 3)
true

julia> unique([2, 3, 5, 2])  # 3 is now deemed equates to 2, hence it should be removed ???
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 2
 3
 5
```

It would be wonderful if we have such a keyword choice:
```julia
julia> a, b = rand(3, 2), rand(3, 2);

julia> norm1(x) = LinearAlgebra.norm(x, 1);

julia> norm1(a .- b)
0.43189600452632304

julia> isapprox(a, b; atol = 0.42, norm = norm1)
false

julia> isapprox(a, b; atol = 0.44, norm = norm1)
true

julia> my_criterion = (a, b) -> isapprox(a, b; atol = 0.42, norm = norm1)
#7 (generic function with 1 method)

julia> my_criterion(a, b)
false

julia> unique([a, b, 2*a, 2*b]; criterion = my_criterion)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching unique(::Vector{Matrix{Float64}}; criterion::var"#7#8")
```

There's  several options for doing a so-so job of it (although, any implementation will be affected by the transitivity issue; so corner cases will be necessarily wonky), with several implementations listed in #19147. E.g., the most naive (and $O(n^2)$ rather than $O(n)$ as for standard `unique`) implementation is:
```jl
function uniquetol(A::AbstractArray{T}; kwargs...) where T
    S = Vector{T}()
    for a in A
         if !any(s -> isapprox(s, a; kwargs...), S)
             push!(S, a)
         end
    end
    return S
end
```
With sorting, this could be changed to $O(n\log n)$. Still, for small, specific problems, it can do a fine job.

For why there isn't a "standard" implementation, see https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/19147#issuecomment-326014320. There is no possible "standard" for this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2478171974/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

have a situation where I'd like to use the [sources] entry of Project.toml, to indicate that a package, X, should first be looked for at some `path` - and, if not present there, be looked for at some `url`. I had hoped this might be possible to do by something like
```toml
[sources]
X = {path="..", url="https://github.com/user/X.jl"}
```
but that's not possible currently, by design, cf. https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/69c6de01909d53b0a4514b11715f829fe0569d98/src/project.jl#L104-L106

I think it would be nice to be able to do this though: it would allow one to develop multiple interdependent private packages locally, preferring local versions specified in `path` if the exist, and then distribute them to collaborators, who might not have a local versions (then pulling from `url`).

Basically, I think it would be natural to let `path` take precedence if both `path` and `url` are present and a directory with a project exists at `path`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4089/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4089/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

@btime Base.copymutable($C); # v1.10.4: 35 ns, PR: 33 ns
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56225/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56225/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2422209706","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56225#issuecomment-2422209706","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56225","id":2422209706,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6QX_yq","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-18T11:04:55Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T11:04:55Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> ```julia

{"action":"opened","number":55748,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55748","id":2068428192,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM57SbWg","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55748","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55748.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55748.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55748","number":55748,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"speed up `collect(::AbstractArray)` by skipping unnecessary alias-check in `copyto!`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"As discussed on Slack with @MasonProtter & @jakobnissen, `collect` currently does a usually cheap - but sometimes expensive - aliasing check (via `unalias`->`mightalias`->`objectid`) before copying contents over; this check is unnecessary, however, since the source array is newly created and cannot possibly alias the input.
This PR fixes that by swapping from `copyto!` to `copyto_unaliased!` in the `_collect_indices` implementations where the swap is straightforward (e.g., it is not so straightforward for the fallback `_collect_indices(indsA, A)`, so I skipped it there).

This improves the following example substantially:
```jl
struct GarbageVector{N} <: AbstractVector{Int}
    v :: Vector{Int}
    garbage :: NTuple{N, Int}
end
GarbageVector{N}(v::Vector{Int}) where N = GarbageVector{N}(v, ntuple(identity, Val(N)))
Base.getindex(gv::GarbageVector, i::Int) = gv.v[i]
Base.size(gv::GarbageVector) = size(gv.v)
Base.iterate(gv::GarbageVector) = iterate(gv.v)
Base.iterate(gv::GarbageVector, state) = iterate(gv.v, state)

# Example with `N = 100`
using BenchmarkTools
v = rand(Int, 10)
gv = GarbageVector{100}(v)
@btime collect($v);  # 30 ns (v1.10.4) -> 30 ns (PR)
@btime collect($gv); # 179 ns (v1.10.4) -> 30 ns (PR)
```

Relatedly, it seems the fact that `mightalias` is comparing immutable contents as well - and hence slowing down the `unalias` check for the above `GarbageVector` via a slow `objectid` on tuples - is suboptimal. I don't know how to fix that though, so I'd like to leave that outside this etc."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2020-01-14T22:32:09Z","updated_at":"2024-09-24T20:38:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Since `reduce` (and friends) are specified to call a two-argument function, the choice of what to do (and what to return) when there's only one element is rather arbitrary and can be surprising.  A great motivating case here is `reduce(vcat, X)`:

```julia
julia> reduce(vcat, [1])
1

julia> reduce(vcat, [1,2])
2-element Array{Int64,1}:
 1
 2
```

I really want `reduce` to just call `vcat(1)`, but of course it cannot blindly do that in general.  The solution is to provide an `init`.  We do patch up this return value in a few notable cases — as this was required to get `reduce` to be type stable.

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/0ee3264be377a70e56465142649e67b4f85dda12/base/reduce.jl#L348-L369

This function is nicely documented but doesn't appear in the manual.  Should we just patch up `vcat` and `hcat` here for now?  Or perhaps `reduce`ing over 1-element collections without an `init` value should be just as much of an error as `reduce`ing over empty ones. ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34380/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34380/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2372341589","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34380#issuecomment-2372341589","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34380","id":2372341589,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6NZw9V","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-24T20:38:32Z","updated_at":"2024-09-24T20:38:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"For the record - and probably obvious to previous posters, but leaving for context for anyone searching - it's not just `vcat` but really any type which is not a monoid over a considered operation. One example is `sum` over a type `X` where `+(::X, ::X)` does not return an `X`, e.g.:

```jl
struct XInt
   v :: Int
end
Base.:+(a::XInt, b::XInt) = a.v + b.v # returns an Int, not an XInt - so not type-closed under addition

vs = XInt.(1:5)
sum(vs[1:1]) # returns an XInt
sum(vs[1:2]) # returns an Int
```
This can be fixed by defining `Base.reduce_first(::typeof(+), v::XInt) = v.v` - but since `Base.reduce_first` is not exported or documented, it seems this is not really a long-term solution.
I'm keen to know if there is a better solution though (aside from ensuring that ones types are closed under addition, which is not always possible or desirable).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2372341589/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```jl
lstr = lazy"hello \
       world"       # == "hello \\\n       world"
str  = "hello \
       world"       # == "hello world"
```

Apart from interpreting the line-continuation character as a "raw" backslash (and consequently also inserting a line-break), `lazy"..."` also doesn't ignore the white-space on the following line. This seems unintended and undesirable. `@lazy_str` macro behaves differently from ordinary strings when using the line continuation character `\`:

```jl
lstr = lazy"hello \
       world"       # == "hello \\\n       world"
str  = "hello \
       world"       # == "hello world"
```

Apart from interpreting the line-continuation character as a "raw" backslash (and consequently also inserting a line-break), `lazy"..."` also doesn't ignore the white-space on the following line. This seems unintended and undesirable. shortens the printing of periods by using abbreviations (e.g. "ns" instead of "nanoseconds").  It also enables parsing of strings that contain the period-type embedded (e.g. "123ns" is parsed as Nanosecond(123)). {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49965","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49965/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49965/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49965/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49965","id":1727972890,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5RdZGe","number":49965,"title":"add method for `getindex(::ProductIterator, inds...)`","user":{"login":"MasonProtter","id":29157027,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5MTU3MDI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/29157027?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter","html_url":"https://github.com/MasonProtter","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":688843543,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2ODg4NDM1NDM=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/triage","name":"triage","color":"e4003c","default":false,"description":"This should be discussed on a triage call"},{"id":815495023,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4MTU0OTUwMjM=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/iteration","name":"iteration","color":"a098ea","default":false,"description":"Involves iteration or the iteration protocol"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2023-05-26T17:11:41Z","updated_at":"2024-01-23T09:57:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49965","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49965","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49965.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49965.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Currently, `ProductIterator` has a rather artificial limitation of not supporting `getindex`, even though there's nothing about `ProductIterator` itself that should preclude it from supporting this function.

It's true that `ProductIterator` **may** contain iterators that don't support indexing, but if that's the case, then it should be the job of the enclosed iterators to throw an error saying they don't support `getindex`, not `ProductIterator`.

Here's some examples of things this enables:
```julia
julia> let itr = Iterators.product(rand(3), rand(3))
           itr[2, 1]
       end
(0.17086814961424035, 0.8039107344409738)

julia> let itr = Iterators.product(Dict(:a => 1, :b => 2), [1, 2, 3])
           itr[:b, 3]
       end
(2, 3)

julia> let itr = Iterators.product()
           itr[]
       end
()
```
and something that rightfully errors:
```julia
julia> let itr = Iterators.product(rand(3), (x for x in 1:10 if rand(Bool)))
           itr[1, 2]
       end
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching getindex(::Base.Generator{Base.Iterators.Filter{var"#10#11", UnitRange{Int64}}, typeof(identity)}, ::Int64)
```
Here, I've implemented the most straightforward case of indexing hoping to solicit comments on what this functionality should offer. For instance, this does not currently support things like linear indexing:
```julia
julia> Iterators.product([:a, :b, :c], ['i', 'j', 'k'])[2, 3]
(:b, 'k')

julia> Iterators.product([:a, :b, :c], ['i', 'j', 'k'])[2]
ERROR: BoundsError: attempt to access Tuple{} at index [1]
```
which I think is okay because this object isn't an abstract array. Thoughts on this direction?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49965/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49965/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1905692367","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49965#issuecomment-1905692367","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49965","id":1905692367,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5xlo7P","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-23T09:57:36Z","updated_at":"2024-01-23T09:57:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think that if Cartesian indexing is implemented for `ProductIterator`, then linear indexing should also be implemented. I.e., it seems to me the following should be allowed:

```jl
julia> P = Iterators.product(1:3, 4:5, 9:10)
julia> A = collect(P)
3×2×2 Array{Tuple{Int64, Int64, Int64}, 3}:
[:, :, 1] =
 (1, 4, 9)  (1, 5, 9)
 (2, 4, 9)  (2, 5, 9)
 (3, 4, 9)  (3, 5, 9)
 
[:, :, 2] =
 (1, 4, 10)  (1, 5, 10)
 (2, 4, 10)  (2, 5, 10)
 (3, 4, 10)  (3, 5, 10)

julia> ijks = (1,2,2)
julia> n = LinearIndices(A)[ijks...] # = 10

julia> A[ijks...] == A[n] # this already holds
julia> P[ijks...] == P[n] = A[ijks...] # this should also hold and be possible
```

An implementation could be borrowed from `Base._ind2sub`:
```jl
getindex(p::ProductIterator, ind::Integer) = getindex(p, CartesianIndex(_ind2sub(size(p), ind)))
getindex(p::ProductIterator, inds::Integer...) = getindex(p, CartesianIndex(inds))
function Base.getindex(p::Iterators.ProductIterator, inds::CartesianIndex)
    length(inds) == length(p.iterators) || prod_indexing_error(p, inds)
    Base.map(getindex, p.iterators, Tuple(inds))
end
```
(although I don't know if the use of `Tuple(::CartesianIndex)` is an antipattern?)

Somewhat relatedly, is it intended that `P[1,2,2,1,1,1,1]` throws while `A[1,2,2,1,1,1,1]` does not? I.e., redundant unit-indices are allowed on indexing of `Array`s but not of `ProductIterator`s.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1905692367/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

adds functions `symmetrize` and `symmetrize!`, which produce symmetric matrices based on the input `X` via efficient computation of `(X + X') / 2`.

Benchmark comparison with a naive implementation:
```
julia> @benchmark symmetrize!(X) setup=(X = randn(20, 20))
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 
  memory estimate:  0 bytes
  allocs estimate:  0
  --------------
  minimum time:     120.718 ns (0.00% GC)
  median time:      121.681 ns (0.00% GC)
  mean time:        124.094 ns (0.00% GC)
  maximum time:     263.909 ns (0.00% GC)
  --------------
  samples:          10000
  evals/sample:     905

julia> f!(X) = (X .= (X .+ X') ./ 2; X)
f! (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @benchmark f!(X) setup=(X = randn(20, 20))
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 
  memory estimate:  3.25 KiB
  allocs estimate:  1
  --------------
  minimum time:     539.921 ns (0.00% GC)
  median time:      587.149 ns (0.00% GC)
  mean time:        668.309 ns (10.85% GC)
  maximum time:     152.017 μs (99.49% GC)
  --------------
  samples:          10000
  evals/sample:     191
```

Currently this only deals with real matrices. ```jl
using StaticArrays, BenchmarkTools

rstack(iter) = reduce(hcat, iter)
iter = SVector{3, SVector{3, Float64}}(SVector{3, Float64}(1.0, 0.0, 0.0), 
                                       SVector{3, Float64}(-0.5, 0.8660254037844387, 0.0), 
                                       SVector{3, Float64}(0.0, 0.0, 0.7923832584290136))

@btime stack($iter);  # 26.788 ns (1 allocation: 80 bytes)
@btime rstack($iter); #  1.800 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

rstack(iter) == stack(iter) # true
```

The code path seems to be via [`_typed_stack(::Colon, ::Float64, ::SVector{3, Float64}, iter)`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/d53fc50c24f5758bd0056f5e3fdf8655da63b9bc/base/abstractarray.jl#L2838). {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1226501235","pull_request_review_id":1474674269,"id":1226501235,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5JGuxz","diff_hunk":"@@ -119,6 +119,10 @@ with both copying and modifying forms (e.g., [`sort`](@ref) and [`sort!`](@ref))
 which are just modifying (e.g., [`push!`](@ref), [`pop!`](@ref), [`splice!`](@ref)).  It
 is typical for such functions to also return the modified array for convenience.
 
+Functions related to IO or making use of random number generators (RNG) are notable exceptions:
+Since these functions almost invariably must mutate the IO or RNG, respectively, functions with ending with `!` are used to signify that an argument _other_ than the IO or RNG is mutated.
+For example, `rand(x)` mutate the RNG, whereas `rand!(x)` mutate the RNG and `x`, and similarly, `read(io)` mutates `io`, whereas `read!(io, x)` mutates both arguments.","path":"doc/src/manual/style-guide.md","commit_id":"6f8c1421bd54e3fc65b3ded940fdcfb4d92dc638","original_commit_id":"6f8c1421bd54e3fc65b3ded940fdcfb4d92dc638","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

{"action":"opened","number":47464,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47464","id":1111800336,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5CRLoQ","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47464","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47464.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47464.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47464","number":47464,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"improve performance of `isapprox(::AbstractArray, ::AbstractArray)` when `rtol = 0`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The default value of `rtol` for `isapprox` depends on whether `atol` is zero or not; if `atol` is nonzero, `rtol` is zero (#22742).

The "normal-path" `isapprox(x, y; atol, rtol` check is just `norm(x-y) <= max(atol, rtol*max(norm(x), norm(y)))`. If `rtol = 0`, however, we are then redundantly computing the norm of both `x` and `y`, which can be expensive for matrices (unlike numbers).

This change simply puts a branch in to check `iszero(rtol)`: if so, it avoids computing the norms redundantly. This speeds up `isapprox(X, Y; atol ≠ 0) considerably:

```jl
julia> A = rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  124.592 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  68.821 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # PR

julia> B = @SMatrix rand(3,3)
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B);
  14.544 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # master
julia> @btime nisapprox($C, $C; atol=1e-11);
  6.900 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # PR
```

The cost of the extra branch is not nothing, of course, but should be negligible in the context of other costs for most array sizes.

---
There's also a small change to the `atol` default value: I noticed that care was being taken to initialize `rtol` to a type consistent with `x` and `y`; I figured the same might as well be done for 0`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The default value of `rtol` for `isapprox` depends on whether `atol` is zero or not; if `atol` is nonzero, `rtol` is zero (#22742).

The "normal-path" `isapprox(x, y; atol, rtol` check is just `norm(x-y) <= max(atol, rtol*max(norm(x), norm(y)))`. If `rtol = 0`, however, we are then redundantly computing the norm of both `x` and `y`, which can be expensive for arrays (unlike numbers).

This change simply puts a branch in to check `iszero(rtol)`: if so, it avoids computing the norms redundantly. This speeds up `isapprox(X, Y; atol ≠ 0)` considerably:

```jl
julia> A = rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  124.592 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  68.821 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # PR

julia> B = @SMatrix rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  14.544 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  6.900 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # PR
```

The cost of the extra branch is not nothing, of course, but should be negligible in the context of other costs for most array sizes.

---
There's also a small change to the `atol` default value: I noticed that care was being taken to initialize `rtol` to a type consistent with `x` and `y`; I figured the same might as well be done for 0`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The default value of `rtol` for `isapprox` depends on whether `atol` is zero or not; if `atol` is nonzero, `rtol` is zero (#22742).

The "normal-path" `isapprox(x, y; atol, rtol` check is just `norm(x-y) <= max(atol, rtol*max(norm(x), norm(y)))`. If `rtol = 0`, however, we are then redundantly computing the norm of both `x` and `y`, which can be expensive for arrays (unlike numbers).

This change simply puts a branch in to check `iszero(rtol)`: if so, it avoids computing the norms redundantly. This speeds up `isapprox(X, Y; atol ≠ 0)` considerably:

```jl
julia> A = rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  124.592 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  68.821 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # PR

julia> B = @SMatrix rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  14.544 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  6.900 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # PR
```

The cost of the extra branch is not nothing, of course, but should be negligible in the context of other costs for most array sizes.

---
There's also a small change to the `atol` default value: I noticed that care was being taken to initialize `rtol` to a type consistent with `x` and `y`; I figured the same might as well be done for 0`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The default value of `rtol` for `isapprox` depends on whether `atol` is zero or not; if `atol` is nonzero, `rtol` is zero (#22742).

The "normal-path" `isapprox(x, y; atol, rtol` check is just `norm(x-y) <= max(atol, rtol*max(norm(x), norm(y)))`. If `rtol = 0`, however, we are then redundantly computing the norm of both `x` and `y`, which can be expensive for arrays (unlike numbers).

This change simply puts a branch in to check `iszero(rtol)`: if so, it avoids computing the norms redundantly. This speeds up `isapprox(X, Y; atol ≠ 0)` considerably:

```jl
julia> A = rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  124.592 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  68.821 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # PR

julia> B = @SMatrix rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  14.544 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  6.900 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # PR
```

The cost of the extra branch is not nothing, of course, but should be negligible in the context of other costs for most array sizes.

---
There's also a small change to the `atol` default value: I noticed that care was being taken to initialize `rtol` to a type consistent with `x` and `y`; I figured the same might as well be done for 0`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The default value of `rtol` for `isapprox` depends on whether `atol` is zero or not; if `atol` is nonzero, `rtol` is zero (#22742).

The "normal-path" `isapprox(x, y; atol, rtol` check is just `norm(x-y) <= max(atol, rtol*max(norm(x), norm(y)))`. If `rtol = 0`, however, we are then redundantly computing the norm of both `x` and `y`, which can be expensive for arrays (unlike numbers).

This change simply puts a branch in to check `iszero(rtol)`: if so, it avoids computing the norms redundantly. This speeds up `isapprox(X, Y; atol ≠ 0)` considerably:

```jl
julia> A = rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  124.592 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  68.821 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # PR

julia> B = @SMatrix rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  14.544 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  6.900 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # PR
```

The cost of the extra branch is not nothing, of course, but should be negligible in the context of other costs for most array sizes.

---
There's also a small change to the `atol` default value: I noticed that care was being taken to initialize `rtol` to a type consistent with `x` and `y`; I figured the same might as well be done for 0`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The default value of `rtol` for `isapprox` depends on whether `atol` is zero or not; if `atol` is nonzero, `rtol` is zero (#22742).

The "normal-path" `isapprox(x, y; atol, rtol` check is just `norm(x-y) <= max(atol, rtol*max(norm(x), norm(y)))`. If `rtol = 0`, however, we are then redundantly computing the norm of both `x` and `y`, which can be expensive for arrays (unlike numbers).

This change simply puts a branch in to check `iszero(rtol)`: if so, it avoids computing the norms redundantly. This speeds up `isapprox(X, Y; atol ≠ 0)` considerably:

```jl
julia> A = rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  124.592 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  68.821 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # PR

julia> B = @SMatrix rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  14.544 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  6.900 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # PR
```

The cost of the extra branch is not nothing, of course, but should be negligible in the context of other costs for most array sizes.

---
There's also a small change to the `atol` default value: I noticed that care was being taken to initialize `rtol` to a type consistent with `x` and `y`; I figured the same might as well be done for performance of `isapprox(::AbstractArray, ::AbstractArray)` when `rtol = 0`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-11-05T22:31:02Z","updated_at":"2022-11-10T02:33:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47464","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47464","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47464.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47464.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"The default value of `rtol` for `isapprox` depends on whether `atol` is zero or not; if `atol` is nonzero, `rtol` is zero (#22742).

The "normal-path" `isapprox(x, y; atol, rtol` check is just `norm(x-y) <= max(atol, rtol*max(norm(x), norm(y)))`. If `rtol = 0`, however, we are then redundantly computing the norm of both `x` and `y`, which can be expensive for arrays (unlike numbers).

This change simply puts a branch in to check `iszero(rtol)`: if so, it avoids computing the norms redundantly. This speeds up `isapprox(X, Y; atol ≠ 0)` considerably:

```jl
julia> A = rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  124.592 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($A, $A; atol=1e-11);
  68.821 ns (1 allocation: 128 bytes) # PR

julia> B = @SMatrix rand(3, 3)
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  14.544 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # master
julia> @btime isapprox($B, $B; atol=1e-11);
  6.900 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes) # PR
```

The cost of the extra branch is not nothing, of course, but should be negligible in the context of other costs for most array sizes.

---
There's also a small change to the `atol` default value: I noticed that care was being taken to initialize `rtol` to a type consistent with `x` and `y`; I figured the same might as well be done for `atol`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47464/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":4,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47464/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1309686278","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47464#issuecomment-1309686278","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47464","id":1309686278,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5OEDoG","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-10T02:33:27Z","updated_at":"2022-11-10T02:33:27Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Okay, that should solve my worry about dynamic dispatch, thanks @LilithHafner.

I think it's fine to keep the more refined `atol` default though, both for style consistency with efforts for `rtol` and to make it likely to do same-type `<=` comparisons.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1309686278/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The current asymmetry between the care taken with the types of `rtol` and `atol` is odd though: in one case, we try quite hard, in the other, not at all.
Incidentally, the explicit `::Real` type-constraints on `rtol` and `atol` seem unfortunate: e.g., this - and the `atol = 0` default makes it so that dimensionful numbers (e.g., `Furlong` or Unitful.jl values) cannot fall back to Base's `isapprox` methods. 
In fact, Unitful.jl seems to currently [copy-extend this entire method](https://github.com/PainterQubits/Unitful.jl/blob/ebad4497360693722c9dae0a6d6f28b5647ad4a6/src/quantities.jl#L294); I assume partly for these reasons (e.g., because a unitful number cannot compare to a non-unitful one; and also because an `AbstractQuantity` isn't a subtype of `Real`).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1309749486/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

PR avoids 1154 - 256 = 898 method invalidations for downstream packages which insert `Base.convert(::Type{String}, ::X)` or similar and adds tests (fixes #47022). Packages which benefit from these changes include `CategoricalArrays.jl` and `ProgressLogging.jl`. 

## `master`

```julia
julia> using SnoopCompileCore

julia> import Base

julia> struct X end

julia> invs = @snoopr Base.convert(::Type{String}, ::X) = X()
[...]

julia> using SnoopCompile

julia> length(uinvalidated(invs))
1154

julia> trees = invalidation_trees(invs)
1-element Vector{SnoopCompile.MethodInvalidations}:
 inserting convert(::Type{String}, ::X) @ Main REPL[3]:1 invalidated:
                 [...]
                 39: signature Tuple{typeof(convert), Type{String}, Any} triggered MethodInstance for REPL.REPLHistoryProvider(::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any) (17 children)
                 [...]
                 42: signature Tuple{typeof(convert), Type{String}, Any} triggered MethodInstance for Base.show_unquoted(::IOContext{Base.TTY}, ::Expr, ::Int64, ::Int64, ::Int64) (82 children)
                 43: signature Tuple{typeof(convert), Type{String}, Any} triggered MethodInstance for Base.CoreLogging.var"#handle_message#2"(::Base.Pairs{Symbol, V, Tuple{Vararg{Symbol, N}}, NamedTuple{names, T}} where {V, N, names, T<:Tuple{Vararg{Any, N}}}, ::typeof(Base.CoreLogging.handle_message), ::Base.CoreLogging.SimpleLogger, ::Base.CoreLogging.LogLevel, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any) (384 children)
                 44: signature Tuple{typeof(convert), Type{String}, Any} triggered MethodInstance for Base.show_unquoted(::IOContext{IOBuffer}, ::Expr, ::Int64, ::Int64, ::Int64) (1311 children)

```julia
julia> using SnoopCompileCore

julia> struct X end

julia> invs = @snoopr Base.convert(::Type{String}, ::X) = X()

julia> using SnoopCompile

julia> length(uinvalidated(invs))
1154
```

We have a global vector of vertices and only store the indices of connectivities to save memory.

You can roll this conversion directly then (or use @mikmoore's suggestion if you need the result to behave like a vector):
```jl
julia> tupleview(v, inds::NTuple{N,Int}) where N = ntuple(i->v[inds[i]], Val(N))

julia> v = rand(100)
julia> inds = (1,3,4)
julia> @btime tupleview($v, $inds)
  1.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
(0.02597032912898689, 0.6171268026598445, 0.8991203209161366)
```

With StaticArrays, this could be:
```
julia> saview(v, inds::NTuple{N,Int}) where N = SVector{N,eltype(v)}(v[ind] for ind in inds)
```
Which compiles to the same thing as `tupleview`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1284410474/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":46573,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46573","id":1043161996,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM4-LWOM","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573","number":46573,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Improve inference of `last(::OneTo{<:Any})`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I was looking at invalidations in StaticArrays and this place came up.
The situation is that there exist some callers of `last` with `OneTo{Any}` which gets inferred as `Any`. An example is `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` (which in turn gets called by some specializations of `similar`, which get invalidated by StaticArrays).

But the allowable typevars in `OneTo{T}` are `T<:Integer`, so we can constrain the result of the access to the field values to `Integer` rather than `Any`. This e.g. lets `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` infer as `Int64` instead of `Any`.

I figured I might as well do the same thing for `first(::OneTo)` while I was at it.

This seems to cut out roughly 260 invalidations from StaticArrays (~about 50% of current invalidations).","created_at":"2022-09-01T03:34:59Z","updated_at":"2022-09-01T03:34:59Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":null,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[],"milestone":null,"draft":false,"commits_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46573/commits","review_comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46573/comments","review_comment_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments{/number}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573/comments","statuses_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/statuses/4e3f136a19b49ec1bf9b2bd05345c790824be40a","head":{"label":"thchr:oneto-inval","ref":"oneto-inval","sha":"4e3f136a19b49ec1bf9b2bd05345c790824be40a","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"repo":{"id":152629239,"node_id":"MDEwOlJlcG9zaXRvcnkxNTI2MjkyMzk=","name":"julia","full_name":"thchr/julia","private":false,"owner":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"h inference of `last(::OneTo{<:Any})`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-09-01T03:34:59Z","updated_at":"2022-09-01T14:13:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46573","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I was looking at invalidations in StaticArrays and this place came up.
The situation is that there exist some callers of `last` with `OneTo{Any}` which gets inferred as `Any`. An example is `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` (which in turn gets called by some specializations of `similar`, which get invalidated by StaticArrays).

But the allowable typevars in `OneTo{T}` are `T<:Integer`, so we can constrain the result of the access to the field values to `Integer` rather than `Any`. This e.g. lets `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` infer as `Int64` instead of `Any`.

I figured I might as well do the same thing for `first(::OneTo)` while I was at it.

This seems to cut out roughly 260 invalidations from StaticArrays (~about 50% of current invalidations).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1234340897","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573#issuecomment-1234340897","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573","id":1234340897,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Jkowh","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-01T14:13:01Z","updated_at":"2022-09-01T14:13:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Or alternatively, we can add type constraint on the method signatures, e.g. `first(r::OneTo{T}) where {T<:Integer} = oneunit(T)`.

The issue with restricting the definition above to e.g. `last(::OneTo{T}) where {T<:Integer}` is that there exist callers of `last(::OneTo{T}) where T`; so if we restrict to `T<:Integer` it seems those callers would still dispatch to `last(r::OrdinalRange{T}) where T` and again infer a return type of `Any` rather than `Integer`.
Examples of such callers are e.g. `similar(::Type{BitArray}, ::Tuple{Union{Integer, OneTo}})` via `Base.to_shape` which originate from `displaysize(::TTY)` on Windows. (I'll submit a PR to suggest a fix for that one in a few minutes though)

> And I guess we should do this automatically in inference, i.e. restrict method signatures using struct type information?

This would be lovely :)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1234340897/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":46577,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46577","id":1043820251,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM4-N27b","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46577","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46577.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46577.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46577","number":46577,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Help inference of uninferred callers of `read(::IO, ::Type{String})`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"There exist several callers of `displaysize(tty::TTY)` with abstractly inferred `tty`. On Windows, this apparently gives to some poorly inferred calls to `materialize` and `similar` which then become an invalidation source.
The issue arises due to an uninferred `String` result of `read` [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/cb5f401aaadcd17fbe366a64afdbf7e6fc71ac69/base/stream.jl#L578).

We can fix this by helping inference see that `read(::IO, ::Type{String})` returns a `String` rather than `Any`:

```jl
# Master
julia> Base.return_types(read, (IO, Type{String}))
1-element Vector{Any}:
 Any

# PR
1-element Vector{Any}:
 String

was looking at invalidations in StaticArrays and this place came up.
The situation is that there exist some callers of `last` with `OneTo{Any}` which gets inferred as `Any`. An example is `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` (which in turn gets called by some specializations of `similar`, which get invalidated by StaticArrays).

But the allowable typevars in `OneTo{T}` are `T<:Integer`, so we can constrain the result of the access to the field values to `Integer` rather than `Any`. This e.g. lets `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` infer as `Int64` instead of `Any`.

I figured I might as well do the same thing for `first(::OneTo)` while I was at it.

This seems to cut out roughly 260 invalidations from StaticArrays (~about 50% of current invalidations).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1234448354","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573#issuecomment-1234448354","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573","id":1234448354,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5JlC_i","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-01T15:33:55Z","updated_at":"2022-09-01T15:33:55Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> That is impossible:

I of course agree that it is impossible to have a `OneTo{T}` where `!(T <: Integer)` - but what I mean is that there seems to be callers which don't know this. I.e., there exist poorly inferred `last(::OneTo{T})` "calls" which don't realize that `T` is constrained to `Integer`.

E.g., I see a method instance for `similar(::Type{BitArray}, ::Tuple{Union{Integer, Base.OneTo}})`: the `OneTo` here doesn't have any constraints on `T` and is equivalent to `OneTo{<:Any}`, right? This then leads to a method instance existing for `last(::OneTo{<:Any})`, even though any run time evaluations of that would have to be `OneTo{<:Integer}`. 
Please correct me if I'm misunderstanding or misphrasing this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1234448354/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Okay, that's a much bigger change, yeah 😮. I went ahead and did that in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573/commits/991600875e7a84295dac4ca1a445ac620f87936e, going through them one by one via
```jl
julia> ms = methods(convert, Tuple{Type{T},T} where T)
julia> for (i,m) in enumerate(ms)
           fn = String(m.file)
           fn = replace(fn, "/cache/build/default-amdci5-5/julialang/julia-master/usr/share/julia/stdlib/v1.9/"=>"/stdlib/")
           b = "/home/tchr/dev/julia" # hardcoded path
           if !startswith(fn, "/stdlib/")
               fn = "/base/"*fn
           end
           edit(b * fn, m.line)
       end
 ```
 
 There were two entries in StaticArrays that I didn't look at since they are no longer in Base.

This change seems related to the discussion in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42372, so I figured I'd cross-link that. inference"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":12,"created_at":"2022-09-01T03:34:59Z","updated_at":"2022-09-02T15:22:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46573","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I was looking at invalidations in StaticArrays and this place came up.
The situation is that there exist some callers of `last` with `OneTo{Any}` which gets inferred as `Any`. An example is `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` (which in turn gets called by some specializations of `similar`, which get invalidated by StaticArrays).

But the allowable typevars in `OneTo{T}` are `T<:Integer`, so we can constrain the result of the access to the field values to `Integer` rather than `Any`. This e.g. lets `Base.to_shape(::OneTo{<:Any})` infer as `Int64` instead of `Any`.

I figured I might as well do the same thing for `first(::OneTo)` while I was at it.

This seems to cut out roughly 260 invalidations from StaticArrays (~about 50% of current invalidations).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1235628349","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46573#issuecomment-1235628349","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46573","id":1235628349,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5JpjE9","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-02T15:22:08Z","updated_at":"2022-09-02T15:22:08Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"There's a test-failure related to the new test which I don't fully understand. Instead of always returning `Any[Integer, Integer]`, the test of `Base.return_types(convert, (Type{Number}, Number))` returns one of the following, depending on the test-run:
```jl
Any[Integer, Integer]
Any[Union{}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Any, Any, Any, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Union{}, Union{}, Union{}, Main.Quaternions.Quaternion, Number, Number]
Any[Union{}, Union{}, Union{}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Any, Any, Any, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Number, Union{}, Number]
Any[Main.Quaternions.Quaternion, Number, Number]
Any[Main.Test76Main_LinearAlgebra_generic.TestGeneric.MyDual, Union{}, Union{}, Main.Quaternions.Quaternion, Union{}, Union{}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Any, Any, Any, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Number, Number]
Any[Main.Test67Main_LinearAlgebra_generic.TestGeneric.MyDual, Union{}, Main.Quaternions.Quaternion, Union{}, Union{}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong{0}, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Any, Any, Any, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Main.Furlongs.Furlong, Number, Union{}, Number]
```

It seems like different `convert` method definition exist in different test-runs?
Any suggestions on how to make this test more robust or whether this is indicative of a real issue with the changes would be welcome!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1235628349/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

end

```jl
# try to print this in a key-sorted fashion while adding a weird overload for matrix print
TOML.print(stdout, d; sorted=true, by=key->parse(Int, key)) do value

end

inference of uninferred callers of `read(::IO, ::Type{String})`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-09-01T14:30:33Z","updated_at":"2022-09-14T21:35:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46577","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46577","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46577.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46577.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"There exist several callers of `displaysize(tty::TTY)` with abstractly inferred `tty`. On Windows, this apparently gives to some poorly inferred calls to `materialize` and `similar` which then become an invalidation source.
The issue arises due to an uninferred `String` result of `read` [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/cb5f401aaadcd17fbe366a64afdbf7e6fc71ac69/base/stream.jl#L578).

We can fix this by helping inference see that `read(::IO, ::Type{String})` returns a `String` rather than `Any`:

```jl
# Master
julia> Base.return_types(read, (IO, Type{String}))
1-element Vector{Any}:
 Any

# PR
julia> Base.return_types(read, (IO, Type{String}))
1-element Vector{Any}:
 String
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46577/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46577/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1247325748","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46577#issuecomment-1247325748","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46577","id":1247325748,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5KWK40","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-14T21:35:22Z","updated_at":"2022-09-14T21:35:22Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Bump :)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1247325748/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Use the EnvCache-ed manifest instead of re-reading the manifest file
2. If the cachefile isn't stale, update its mtime. This is already done in base. 
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/0bd886297d2757adf75d346a7dbb438fd839f8e6/base/loading.jl#L753-L756
While I'm not sure why it was originally added, now that cachefiles are sorted by mtime during evaluation, there is the additional benefit of keeping the `mtime` of valid cache files up to date

More info: the cache file is `touch`-ed in base during code load to make the `mtime` of the cache more representative of last used time. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46875","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46875/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46875/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46875/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46875","id":1384155274,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5SgIiK","number":46875,"title":"Parsing of `.field` and `.property` syntax allows space","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-09-23T18:18:26Z","updated_at":"2022-09-23T18:18:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I was surprised to see that this is allowed:
```jl
julia> struct A
           a :: Int
       end

julia> A(1).             a
1
```
I.e., the lowered code just disregards the spaces:
```jl
julia> @code_lowered A(1).            a
CodeInfo(
1 ─      nothing
│   %2 = Base.getfield(x, f)
└──      return %2
)
```
A similar thing happens for `getproperty`.

I imagine that it is not a good idea to fix/change this in Julia v1.x, since there could well be plenty of typos out there that implicitly depend on this - but I was suggested to file an issue for it anyway; at least there's a record of the thought this way.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46875/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46875/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}
