latency"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2021-01-11T16:50:57Z","updated_at":"2022-09-25T01:36:16Z","closed_at":"2021-03-08T14:09:33Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/39194","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39194","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39194.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39194.patch","merged_at":"2021-03-08T14:09:33Z"},"body":"It makes sense to extract the axes before we lose inferrability
of the vector or matrix being printed, because the type-diversity
of axes should be vastly lower than that of arrays. Hence this delays
application of `@nospecialize`. However, it also standardizes
the row/column indices and reduces specialization in `alignment`,
so the net effect should be fewer distinct MethodInstances.

@dlfivefifty, any chance you could test this and see if it breaks anything for you?
The automatic concatenation on `rowsA` and `colsA` seems worth checking.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39194/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39194/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1257097372","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39194#issuecomment-1257097372","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39194","id":1257097372,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5K7cic","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-25T01:36:16Z","updated_at":"2022-09-25T01:36:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The inferencebarriers and nospecialize on `print_matrix` and `_show_output` here appear to cause invalidations for anything called with the uninferred arguments `X`. In particular, this seems to affect extensions of `isassigned` (e.g. StaticArrays extensions of `isassigned` invalidates this code since it includes callers of `isassigned(::AbsVecOrMat), :Int...)`, see https://github.com/JuliaArrays/StaticArrays.jl/pull/1096#issuecomment-1257059816).

I'm wondering if there is a way to avoid this? I understand these barriers were added for the sake of improving latency by aiding precompilation - so I'm mainly wondering if there also is a way to still avoid the inadvertent invalidations resulting from this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1257097372/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```jl
# create a Dict whose values cannot be printed by TOML.print by default
d = Dict("4"=>[4 4], "1"=>[1 1], "-2"=>[2 2], "3"=>[3 3])

# try to print this in a key-sorted fashion while adding a weird overload for matrix print
TOML.print(stdout, d; sorted=true, by=key->parse(Int, key)) do value
   Dict("x"=>value[1], "y"=>value[2])
end
```

The example above works fine if I drop the `by` keyword or if I don't include two variables in the `Dict` created in the `to_toml` function. E.g., the following works fine:

```jl
# try to print this in a key-sorted fashion while adding a weird overload for matrix print
TOML.print(stdout, d; sorted=true, by=key->parse(Int, key)) do value
   Dict("x"=>value[1])
end

TOML.print(stdout, d) do value
   Dict("x"=>value[1], "y"=>value[2])
end

notice that we use both `kws...` and `kw...` with little apparent pattern. I prefer using one consistently, so I changed almost all occurrences of `kws...` to `kw...`. I chose `kw` because it is already more common.
```
grep -owr 'kws\.\.\.' base stdlib test | wc -l
      77
grep -owr 'kw\.\.\.' base stdlib test | wc -l 
     160
```

Most of this pr comes from the command 
```
grep -rlw 'kws\.\.\.' base stdlib/Sockets stdlib/Test stdlib/Markdown stdlib/REPL stdlib/InteractiveUtils/ stdlib/LinearAlgebra/ stdlib/Logging/ test | xargs sed -I '' 's/kws\.\.\./kw.../'
```
I only dealt with stdlibs that are a part of the julia repo and contain the string notice that we use both `kws...` and `kw...` with little apparent pattern. I prefer using one consistently, so I changed almost all occurrences of `kws...` to `kw...`. I chose `kw` because it is already more common.
```
grep -owr 'kws\.\.\.' base stdlib test | wc -l
      77
grep -owr 'kw\.\.\.' base stdlib test | wc -l 
     160
```

Most of this pr comes from the command 
```
grep -rlw 'kws\.\.\.' base stdlib/Sockets stdlib/Test stdlib/Markdown stdlib/REPL stdlib/InteractiveUtils/ stdlib/LinearAlgebra/ stdlib/Logging/ test | xargs sed -I '' 's/kws\.\.\./kw.../'
```
I only dealt with stdlibs that are a part of the julia repo and contain the string * [...] Commenters here have alluded to StaticArrays needing this type in some cases, but I don't quite get it yet. I would like to see that spelled out in a detailed example.

I think one prominent example is the following (as discussed e.g. at https://github.com/JuliaArrays/StaticArrays.jl/issues/807): in order to encode the size `S` of a `StaticArray{S, ...}` type, it is currently the tuple-type `S = Tuple{N, M, ...}`. One unappealing consequence of this is that `S` doesn't have an instance. If we had `StaticInt`, `S` could be a `Tuple{StaticInt{N}, StaticInt{M}, ...}` which would have the instance `(StaticInt(N), StaticInt(M), ...)`.
This would allow StaticArrays.jl to drop some of the awkward juggling between `Size` types and `size`: with a `StaticInt`, we could have `size(::StaticArray)` return this instance in a natural manner (and so keep the static information around; right now, it simply returns `(N, M, ...)` and so drops the compile-time information; to retain the static information, one currently needs to use `Size`).

(Of course, this would be quite breaking on the StaticArrays.jl side...)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1164886488/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

compat "Julia x.y" to the docstring"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":23,"created_at":"2022-03-29T20:34:17Z","updated_at":"2022-04-08T00:05:55Z","closed_at":"2022-04-07T07:39:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44792","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44792","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44792.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44792.patch","merged_at":"2022-04-07T07:39:47Z"},"body":"Related to #44294


flatmap is the composition of map and flatten. It is important for functional programming patterns.

Some tasks that can be easily attained with list-comprehensions, including the filter-mapping ([f(x) for x in xx if p(x)]), or flattening a list of computed lists, can only be attained with do-syntax style if a flatmap functor is available. (Or appending a `|> flatten`, etc.)

Filtering can be implemented by outputting empty lists or singleton lists for the values to be removed or kept. A more proper approach would be the optional monad, though, usually implemented in Julia as a union of Some and Nothing.

This patch therefore also implements iteration methods for Some and Nothing, to enable the filter-map pattern with flatmap.

Highlights:
 - A `flatmap` over a collection of optionals is not equivalent to a composition of map and filter.
 - do-syntax today is limited compared to comprehensions or for-loops with continues and appends. Offering flatmap and flattening optionals brings do-syntax closer to these other constructs.
 - Flattening over optionals, and further applying `map` etc, is necessary to make Some-Nothing equivalent to optional monads in other languages, from Haskell and Scala to C++17. Right now a `map(sqrt, Some(4))` wouldn't work in Julia, though. Is this something we would like to pursue?
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44792/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":1,"-1":1,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44792/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1092315518","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44792#issuecomment-1092315518","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44792","id":1092315518,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5BG2l-","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-08T00:05:55Z","updated_at":"2022-04-08T00:05:55Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Little bit late to the game but: how is `flatmap(f, c...)` an improvement syntactically or expression-wise over the very simple and already quite short `flatten(map(f, c...))` (the implementation)?

I.e. this seems to just add another short verb for a nearly trivial composition of two more basic verbs?

Bit grouchy, I realize, but from reading the discussion it was not clear to me why it is preferable to introduce a new function for this essentially trivial composition (unless there's e.g. a performance argument)?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1092315518/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44792/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":1,"-1":1,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44792/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1092910960","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44792#issuecomment-1092910960","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44792","id":1092910960,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5BJH9w","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-08T14:17:56Z","updated_at":"2022-04-08T14:17:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Having `flatmap` defined you can write do-blocks in a more natural way.

But composition already allows you to write this equivalently as:
```jl
(flatten ∘ map)(1:3) do j
    (flatten ∘ map)(1:3) do k
        j!=k ? ((j,k),) : ()
    end
end
```
On the face of it, you save 8 characters for every `(flatten ∘ map)` you replace by `flatmap`; but in the above example, that's a meager 17% reduction of the total `do`-block's length (in any nontrivial example, it seems likely the savings would be even smaller).

> And in fact, I would often forget that `flatmap` even exists, so it's a bit of a PSA thing as well.

I think this is my main counterpoint to adding functions like this: it increases the vocabulary of the language and reduces the chance that everyone can read a bit code without needing to look up functions in the "dictionary"/docs. 
I think it is worthwhile to add new "words" to Base if (a) things _cannot_ be done simply and briefly in terms of existing words or (b) if the new word enables performance benefits; but neither seems to apply here?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1092910960/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+"""
+    StaticInt(N::Int) -> StaticInt{N}()
+
+A statically sized `Int`. Use `StaticInt(N)` instead of `Val(N)` when you want it to behave like a number.
+"""
+struct StaticInt{N} <: Integer
+    StaticInt{N}() where {N} = new{N::Int}()
+    StaticInt(N::Int) = StaticInt{N}()
+    StaticInt(N::StaticInt) = N
+    StaticInt(::Val{N}) where {N} = StaticInt(N)
+    StaticInt(N) = StaticInt(Int(N))
+end
+
+_dynamic_int(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt))::Int = _dynamic_int(typeof(x))
+_dynamic_int(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt}))::Int = x.parameters[1]
+
+const Zero = StaticInt{0}
+const One = StaticInt{1}
+
+Base.convert(::Type{T}, @nospecialize(N::StaticInt)) where {T<:Number} = convert(T, Int(N))
+Base.Bool(x::StaticInt{0}) = false
+Base.Bool(x::StaticInt{1}) = true
+
+Base.BigInt(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = BigInt(Int(x))
+Base.Integer(x::StaticInt) = x
+(::Type{T})(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) where {T<:Integer} = T(_dynamic_int(x))
+(::Type{T})(x::Int) where {T<:StaticInt} = StaticInt(x)
+Base.convert(::Type{StaticInt{N}}, ::StaticInt{N}) where {N} = StaticInt{N}()
+
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{T2}) where {T2<:Number} = promote_type(Int, T2)
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{T2}) where {T2<:AbstractIrrational} = promote_type(Int, T2)
+for (S, T) in [(:Complex, :Real), (:Rational, :Integer), (:(Base.TwicePrecision), :Any)]
+    @eval function Base.promote_rule(::Type{$S{T}}, @nospecialize(SI::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T<:$T}
+        promote_type($S{T}, Int)
+    end
+end
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{Union{Nothing,Missing}}, @nospecialize(T::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Union{Nothing,Missing,Int}
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Union{Missing,Nothing}} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Nothing} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Missing} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+for T in [:Bool, :Missing, :BigFloat, :BigInt, :Nothing, :Any]
+    # let S = :Any
+    @eval begin
+        Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(S::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{$T}) = promote_type(Int, $T)
+        Base.promote_rule(::Type{$T}, @nospecialize(S::Type{<:StaticInt})) = promote_type($T, Int)
+    end
+end
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Int
+
+Base.:(%)(@nospecialize(n::StaticInt), ::Type{Integer}) = Int(n)
+
+Base.eltype(@nospecialize(T::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Int
+Base.iszero(::Zero) = true
+Base.iszero(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = false
+Base.isone(::One) = true
+Base.isone(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = false
+Base.zero(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Zero()
+Base.one(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt})) = One()
+
+for T in [:Real, :Rational, :Integer]
+    @eval begin
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::$T, y::Zero) = x
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x + Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::Zero, y::$T) = x
+        @inline Base.:(+)(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt), y::$T) = Int(x) + y
+        @inline Base.:(-)(x::$T, y::Zero) = x
+        @inline Base.:(-)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x - Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, y::Zero) = Zero()
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, y::One) = x
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x * Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::Zero, y::$T) = Zero()
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::One, y::$T) = y
+        @inline Base.:(*)(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt), y::$T) = Int(x) * y
+    end
+end
+@inline Base.:(+)(::Zero, ::Zero) = Zero()
+@inline Base.:(+)(::Zero, ::StaticInt{M}) where {M} = StaticInt{M}()

+@inline Base.:(*)(::One, ::Zero) = Zero()
+@inline Base.:(*)(::Zero, ::One) = Zero()
+@inline Base.:(*)(::One, ::One) = One()
+@inline Base.:(*)(::StaticInt{M}, ::Zero) where {M} = Zero()
+@inline Base.:(*)(::Zero, ::StaticInt{M}) where {M} = Zero()
+@inline Base.:(*)(::StaticInt{M}, ::One) where {M} = StaticInt{M}()
+@inline Base.:(*)(::One, ::StaticInt{M}) where {M} = StaticInt{M}()

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/822839573","pull_request_review_id":904636789,"id":822839573,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4xC4kV","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,111 @@
+
+"""
+    StaticInt(N::Int) -> StaticInt{N}()
+
+A statically sized `Int`. Use `StaticInt(N)` instead of `Val(N)` when you want it to behave like a number.
+"""
+struct StaticInt{N} <: Integer
+    StaticInt{N}() where {N} = new{N::Int}()
+    StaticInt(N::Int) = StaticInt{N}()
+    StaticInt(N::StaticInt) = N
+    StaticInt(::Val{N}) where {N} = StaticInt(N)
+    StaticInt(N) = StaticInt(Int(N))
+end
+
+_dynamic_int(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt))::Int = _dynamic_int(typeof(x))
+_dynamic_int(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt}))::Int = x.parameters[1]
+
+const Zero = StaticInt{0}
+const One = StaticInt{1}
+
+Base.convert(::Type{T}, @nospecialize(N::StaticInt)) where {T<:Number} = convert(T, Int(N))
+Base.Bool(x::StaticInt{0}) = false
+Base.Bool(x::StaticInt{1}) = true
+
+Base.BigInt(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = BigInt(Int(x))
+Base.Integer(x::StaticInt) = x
+(::Type{T})(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) where {T<:Integer} = T(_dynamic_int(x))
+(::Type{T})(x::Int) where {T<:StaticInt} = StaticInt(x)
+Base.convert(::Type{StaticInt{N}}, ::StaticInt{N}) where {N} = StaticInt{N}()
+
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{T2}) where {T2<:Number} = promote_type(Int, T2)
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{T2}) where {T2<:AbstractIrrational} = promote_type(Int, T2)
+for (S, T) in [(:Complex, :Real), (:Rational, :Integer), (:(Base.TwicePrecision), :Any)]
+    @eval function Base.promote_rule(::Type{$S{T}}, @nospecialize(SI::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T<:$T}
+        promote_type($S{T}, Int)
+    end
+end
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{Union{Nothing,Missing}}, @nospecialize(T::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Union{Nothing,Missing,Int}
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Union{Missing,Nothing}} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Nothing} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Missing} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+for T in [:Bool, :Missing, :BigFloat, :BigInt, :Nothing, :Any]
+    # let S = :Any
+    @eval begin
+        Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(S::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{$T}) = promote_type(Int, $T)
+        Base.promote_rule(::Type{$T}, @nospecialize(S::Type{<:StaticInt})) = promote_type($T, Int)
+    end
+end
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Int
+
+Base.:(%)(@nospecialize(n::StaticInt), ::Type{Integer}) = Int(n)
+
+Base.eltype(@nospecialize(T::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Int
+Base.iszero(::Zero) = true
+Base.iszero(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = false
+Base.isone(::One) = true
+Base.isone(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = false
+Base.zero(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Zero()
+Base.one(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt})) = One()
+
+for T in [:Real, :Rational, :Integer]
+    @eval begin
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::$T, y::Zero) = x
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x + Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::Zero, y::$T) = x
+        @inline Base.:(+)(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt), y::$T) = Int(x) + y
+        @inline Base.:(-)(x::$T, y::Zero) = x
+        @inline Base.:(-)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x - Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, y::Zero) = Zero()
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, y::One) = x
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x * Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::Zero, y::$T) = Zero()
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::One, y::$T) = y
+        @inline Base.:(*)(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt), y::$T) = Int(x) * y
+    end
+end","path":"base/staticint.jl","position":76,"original_position":76,"commit_id":"9322676871be8fecedd7ae5f24e1f19814eb70d4","original_commit_id":"9322676871be8fecedd7ae5f24e1f19814eb70d4","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Something similar to my other comment applies to this block of code: it seems many of the `Zero` and `One` specific definitions could be dropped?
I see there's the added complexity of wanting to `@nospecialize` the generic `StaticInt` arguments here - but wouldn't it be reasonable to have similar desires for `One` and `Zero`?

A related question: the current design returns `Zero` and `One` for mixed-operations where it is is possible to statically determine that the output will be a `Zero` or `One` (e.g., `(*)(x::Zero, y::$T)`). That's neat, definitely, but it does seem a little subtle. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/822865087","pull_request_review_id":904672239,"id":822865087,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4xC-y_","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,111 @@
+
+"""
+    StaticInt(N::Int) -> StaticInt{N}()
+
+A statically sized `Int`. Use `StaticInt(N)` instead of `Val(N)` when you want it to behave like a number.
+"""
+struct StaticInt{N} <: Integer
+    StaticInt{N}() where {N} = new{N::Int}()
+    StaticInt(N::Int) = StaticInt{N}()
+    StaticInt(N::StaticInt) = N
+    StaticInt(::Val{N}) where {N} = StaticInt(N)
+    StaticInt(N) = StaticInt(Int(N))
+end
+
+_dynamic_int(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt))::Int = _dynamic_int(typeof(x))
+_dynamic_int(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt}))::Int = x.parameters[1]
+
+const Zero = StaticInt{0}
+const One = StaticInt{1}
+
+Base.convert(::Type{T}, @nospecialize(N::StaticInt)) where {T<:Number} = convert(T, Int(N))
+Base.Bool(x::StaticInt{0}) = false
+Base.Bool(x::StaticInt{1}) = true
+
+Base.BigInt(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = BigInt(Int(x))
+Base.Integer(x::StaticInt) = x
+(::Type{T})(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) where {T<:Integer} = T(_dynamic_int(x))
+(::Type{T})(x::Int) where {T<:StaticInt} = StaticInt(x)
+Base.convert(::Type{StaticInt{N}}, ::StaticInt{N}) where {N} = StaticInt{N}()
+
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{T2}) where {T2<:Number} = promote_type(Int, T2)
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{T2}) where {T2<:AbstractIrrational} = promote_type(Int, T2)
+for (S, T) in [(:Complex, :Real), (:Rational, :Integer), (:(Base.TwicePrecision), :Any)]
+    @eval function Base.promote_rule(::Type{$S{T}}, @nospecialize(SI::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T<:$T}
+        promote_type($S{T}, Int)
+    end
+end
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{Union{Nothing,Missing}}, @nospecialize(T::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Union{Nothing,Missing,Int}
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Union{Missing,Nothing}} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Nothing} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+Base.promote_rule(::Type{T1}, @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) where {T1>:Missing} = promote_type(T1, Int)
+for T in [:Bool, :Missing, :BigFloat, :BigInt, :Nothing, :Any]
+    # let S = :Any
+    @eval begin
+        Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(S::Type{<:StaticInt}), ::Type{$T}) = promote_type(Int, $T)
+        Base.promote_rule(::Type{$T}, @nospecialize(S::Type{<:StaticInt})) = promote_type($T, Int)
+    end
+end
+Base.promote_rule(@nospecialize(T1::Type{<:StaticInt}), @nospecialize(T2::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Int
+
+Base.:(%)(@nospecialize(n::StaticInt), ::Type{Integer}) = Int(n)
+
+Base.eltype(@nospecialize(T::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Int
+Base.iszero(::Zero) = true
+Base.iszero(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = false
+Base.isone(::One) = true
+Base.isone(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt)) = false
+Base.zero(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt})) = Zero()
+Base.one(@nospecialize(x::Type{<:StaticInt})) = One()
+
+for T in [:Real, :Rational, :Integer]
+    @eval begin
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::$T, y::Zero) = x
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x + Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(+)(x::Zero, y::$T) = x
+        @inline Base.:(+)(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt), y::$T) = Int(x) + y
+        @inline Base.:(-)(x::$T, y::Zero) = x
+        @inline Base.:(-)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x - Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, y::Zero) = Zero()
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, y::One) = x
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::$T, @nospecialize(y::StaticInt)) = x * Int(y)
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::Zero, y::$T) = Zero()
+        @inline Base.:(*)(x::One, y::$T) = y
+        @inline Base.:(*)(@nospecialize(x::StaticInt), y::$T) = Int(x) * y
+    end
+end","path":"base/staticint.jl","position":76,"original_position":76,"commit_id":"9322676871be8fecedd7ae5f24e1f19814eb70d4","original_commit_id":"9322676871be8fecedd7ae5f24e1f19814eb70d4","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"FWIW, I just checked what the `@code_llvm` of just doing it the plain way (i.e., not specializing to `Zero` etc.) for e.g. `(+)(::Int, ::Zero)` is: there's no difference between specializing it manually and letting the compiler do it.

```jl
julia> using StaticArrays
julia> x = [(@MMatrix randn(3,2)), (@MMatrix randn(2,3))]
julia> typeof(x)
Vector{MArray{S, Float64, 2, 6} where S<:Tuple} (alias for Array{MArray{S, Float64, 2, 6} where S<:Tuple, 1})
```

On current master/nightly, the same invocation gives:
```jl
julia> typeof(x)
Vector{Matrix{Float64}} (alias for Array{Array{Float64, 2}, 1})
```

I don't know if this was intended, but it seems a little much to simply promote the eltype to `Matrix{Float64}` (I would've thought just having it as `Any` would be preferable).

Found while investigating a [failure](https://github.com/JuliaArrays/StaticArrays.jl/runs/5522395851?check_suite_focus=true) on nightly in StaticArrays' test-suite.

We define some type and overload `Base.show` for it, using `printstyled` to get some color in the print out:
```jl
struct StyledType
    x::Vector{Int}
end

function Base.show(io::IO, ct::StyledType)
    print(io, ct.x)
    printstyled(io, " +color", color=:red)
end

ct = StyledType(1:3)
```
where `ct` then prints as:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26799210/159786117-34eeb82d-a7c5-4e56-b9ef-a012803022c9.png)

But if we now put `ct` elements into a Dict, we get issues with the color when the Dict entries are truncated:
```jl
d = Dict(n => fill(StyledType(1:n), n) for n = 1:5)
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26799210/159786302-1868c686-0cda-40d9-9be5-6c501da5b58b.png)

+Calls to `@deprecate` without explicit type-annotations will define deprecated methods
+accepting arguments of type `Any`. To restrict deprecation to a specific signature, annotate
+the arguments of `old`. For example,
+```jldoctest
+julia> new(x::Int) = x
+
+julia> new(x::Float64) = 2x
+
+julia> @deprecate old(x::Int) new(x)

+```","path":"base/deprecated.jl","position":null,"original_position":29,"commit_id":"ba4344658a7ce6efa0b22982ea4735d4404f7e77","original_commit_id":"6bad66c36c224da64685e2446170d2d165bcdcc6","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Ah, +# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> iroot(100, 2)
+10
+
+julia> iroot(99,2)
+9
+```

adds a new method `cd(m::Module)` that sets the current working directory to `m` package root; if `m` is not a package, an error is thrown.

Sure, a plain error may be better: will do.

> This feels a bit strange to me. What is the usecase for `cd`ing to a module like this?

I have at least two use cases, personally: 
1. Just general exploration of package structure and `/src/` files at the REPL; essentially a convenience to avoid needing to leave the REPL and go to the shell (where it is tricky to find the package one is looking at).
2. Running parts of a package's test individually by `include("test/[some-test].jl")`. I find that helpful when I'm trying to debug a test error and want the program state available for inspection.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1016758880/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
